summary: Gruber reacts to the bombshell news of Jony Ive's departure from Apple, arguing that the spin about Ive continuing as an independent consultant is just that — spin. He contends that Ive's post-Jobs tenure saw hardware design prioritize thinness over function (notably the MacBook keyboard disaster) and software design decline, comparing the Ive-Jobs partnership to Lennon-McCartney. While acknowledging Ive's extraordinary legacy including San Francisco typeface and Apple Park, Gruber's core concern is not that Ive is leaving but that Apple isn't replacing the chief design officer role, leaving design reporting to operations rather than a dedicated design leader.
key_points: The framing of Ive as an independent consultant still working with Apple is spin — he's effectively gone, and no one in media got advance notice
Ive's departure may be good news, as his post-Jobs obsession with thinness likely caused the MacBook keyboard fiasco and software design declined under his watch
The Ive-Jobs partnership produced results far greater than either could alone, like McCartney without Lennon

Ive's legacy includes extraordinary achievements in consistency — San Francisco typeface for everything, unified architectural design language — but his attention had shifted from computers to architecture
The real danger is not Ive leaving but the lack of a replacement CDO, with design now reporting to operations under Jeff Williams
money_quotes: You're either at Apple or you're not. Ive is out.
Today's MacBooks are worse computers but more beautiful devices than the ones they replaced.
Fuck this 'sir' shit. We don't have titles in the United States.
My gut sense for years has been that Ive without Jobs has been like McCartney without Lennon.
The entire point of Apple has always been and should always remain designing computers. Everything they make is a computer.
We've got computers — excellent computers — in our fucking ears. That's Apple.
Apple doesn't need a chief architect. They're only going to build one Apple Park and it's already been done.
I don't worry that Apple is in trouble because Jony Ive is leaving; I worry that Apple is in trouble because he's not being replaced.
key_insight: The real threat to Apple isn't losing Jony Ive — it's the organizational decision not to replace the chief design officer role, leaving design subordinate to operations rather than led by a dedicated tastemaker.
